data.title: Sally Beauty: Ring The Register On These Gains, Run A House Position
data.description:
Today we are following up on Sally Beauty Holdings, Inc. (SBH). This is a stock we have held a small house position in. What is a house position? This is something we teach at BAD BEAT Investing. Essentially, you close a highly profitable trade but leave a chunk of the profit in a name long-term. We like this approach for a long-term wealth-building strategy and discuss it heavily at BAD BEAT Investing. We moved on SBH stock and reissued a buy when it was in the $9s, and reiterated a buy call in August a bit over $11. Today shares are over $16. We think it is a good place to take profit if you took those trades, and leave a small position on as a house position. A very nice trading return has been had this year, and assuming you bought twice, gains are in the 50% range depending on the average basis following our two buy calls this year. Running a house position is an option for those who took the trade and think the stock can edge higher. There remains high short interest here, which is still a headwind. However, performance operationally is improving. Let us discuss the recently reported earnings.

Sally Beauty Holdings Headlines Fiscal Q4 Results

Make no mistake, the fiscal Q4 earnings were better than expected. There was a top line beat versus consensus expectations. Consensus expected a sales decline, but revenue came in with a year-over-year increase. The top-line sales figure came in at $947 million. We had anticipated flat year-over-year sales. Instead, sales were up 1.3% from a year ago, slightly better than we expected, and it surpassed consensus by a notable $14 million.

Of course, when examining any retailer, the key metric we like to look at is comparable sales. With sales up, it was not surprising to see comps also rise in Q4. We were looking for a comparable sales print up 0.5%-0.7%. Instead, we saw consolidated comparable sales rise by 1.3%. This was well above expectations. Another thing to keep in mind is that Sally Beauty has shuttered hundreds of stores in the past few years, so, this explains why we expected higher comps but flat sales overall. Since comps were better than expected, revenues were better than expected. At the same time, e-commerce sales were 11.1% of overall sales, and continue to slowly make up more of the sales mix. We still think that you will see a double-digit percentage of overall sales being made online as we move forward.

Now, comps drove sales higher; we also watched another key metric in margins. We saw improvement in margins as well, which gives confidence in holding a smaller position of profit for more long-term gains. Consolidated gross profit was $494.1 million, versus $479.2 million last year, rising 3.1%. Gross margin was 52.2%, an increase of 100 basis points compared to 51.2% last year, and this was also a sequential increase of 70 basis points. And for the year 2025, adjusted gross margin was up 80 basis points to 51.7%. Very strong results.

Adjusted operating earnings were $89 million, up from $3 million sequentially. We saw an adjusted operating margin that expanded to 9.4% from 9.2% sequentially and year-over-year. All of this combined to result in expanding net income, which came in at $49.9 million, up 3.9% from $48.1 million last year. On a per-share basis, we saw $0.51 in EPS, and as adjusted EPS was up 10% from a year ago to $0.55, beating expectations by $0.07. Thus, comps are up, sales are up, margins are up, and earnings are up. The company has continued to turn the quarter nicely. Considering the valuation, shares are still reasonably valued, though not nearly as attractive as when we rated shares a buy at $9 and $11. Still, we think shares could edge higher if they continue to surpass expectations. So we like holding a house position on a trade.

Looking Ahead

Adding to the positive operational results is the notable balance sheet improvement, with free cash flow of $78 million in the quarter. Sally Beauty ended the quarter with $149 million of cash and equivalents, and there are no borrowings on its revolving credit facility. It was a nice sequential improvement as well from $113 million in cash. The net debt leverage ratio came down to 1.6X, down sequentially from 1.7X and 1.8X at the end of Q2 this year. Things are improving, folks. The company in Q4 had also repaid $21 million of term loan B debt and repurchased 1.7 million shares under its share repurchase program for $20 million.

As we look ahead to fiscal 2026, the company guided for modest gains. They see flat comparable sales to up 1% for the fiscal year, which suggests net sales of $3.745 billion at the midpoint. Further, management sees free cash flow of $200 million, and from an EPS standpoint, $2.05 at the midpoint. At $16 per share, this is just under 8X FWD EPS, which remains cheap, though we note the growth rate is slow, which may suggest the stock is trading at an appropriate multiple for the growth. As such, we like closing out the trade and considering a small house position of profit.

data.title: SLVR: The Silver Bull Run Will Continue
data.description:
My previous article on silver contained a buy call for SILJ) at around $16.50 per share earlier this year. I am still bullish on the metal and think that it has more room to run even higher. However, I have since pivoted from junior miners to SLVR which contains more mature companies in the mix. I believe that SLVR will benefit from higher risk-adjusted returns in the future as these larger companies report higher earnings from a higher silver spot price. Despite the hefty spot price of silver, I'm confident that it will continue to make higher highs in the future.

Overview

The Sprott Silver Miners & Physical Silver ETF (SLVR) is one of the newer silver ETFs to reach the market, having been launched on Jan 14 this year. It provides investors exposure to both silver mining companies and direct exposure to the underlying metal through using silver trusts.

The ETF currently has around 15% of its total concentration in physical silver through trusts, while the rest is dedicated to companies involved in the silver industry itself. SLVR's expense ratio is 0.65%, and it has an upcoming, semi-annual rebalancing date of Dec 22.

SLVR is passively-managed and it seeks to track the Nasdaq Sprott Silver Miners Index (NSLVR). The index is comprised of silver producers, explorers, and developers and follows a free-float market cap constitution. This means that in practice, SLVR's portfolio is concentrated into two big silver miners, namely First Majestic and Endeavour Silver Corp., while also having a large weight in the Sprott Physical Silver Trust (PSLV) as its method of gaining direct exposure to the underlying.

I personally prefer SLVR's approach to investing in small-to-medium-sized silver mining companies over direct silver exposure. All else being equal, equity-based silver ETFs that invest in companies have had a stronger total return performance than investing in ETFs that track that underlying, such as (SLV). Indeed, over the last 12 months, SLVR outperformed SLV by nearly 50%. I expect that this trend will continue.

Thesis

My August article touched on the market deficit for silver, and that deficit has continued for the fifth straight year. My view now is that silver is becoming more akin to an industrial metal like copper or nickel than strictly adjacent to gold as a precious metal. This is due to the metal's rising importance in the manufacture of high-end electronic components used in the solar, electric vehicle, and semiconductor industries.

There are two reasons why I am bullish on SLVR as my preferred silver ETF and for the metal in general. The first is that although industrial demand for silver could be set to rise to a new high above 700 million ounces in 2026, supply is already in a deficit, and I expect this deficit to continue. Unlike gold, silver production is usually a byproduct of mining other metals such as lead or copper. Expanding new primary silver mines takes years and a lot of capital in what has long been seen as a speculative market. That may no longer be the case today, but it means that unlike in other markets, the supply of silver can take a long time to reach parity with its spot price, even in historic rallies as we saw this year. The supply and demand imbalance is a unique structural element of the silver market, and is a key reason why I think we'll see the long-term average price of the metal find steady support.

The second reason why I am bullish on SLVR specifically is that the metal reached multi-decade highs this year, and over the next couple of quarters, companies that SLVR tracks will be releasing their earnings, and I expect them to be very positive both in terms of their reported earnings (due to the bull run) and guidance moving forward (due to supply imbalances and demand tailwinds). Investors can arguably benefit more from this expected tailwind by holding SLVR over junior miners tracked by SILJ or the metal itself via SLV. SLVR tracks larger and more established companies than junior miners, so it makes sense to me that they have been better positioned to take a larger share of value from the continued rally.

Peer Comparison

Comparing SLVR against its peers, I have chosen a group that includes SILJ, AGMI, SIL, and SLVP.

Although SLVP has a stronger long-term performance than SLVR, Sprott's offering combines both silver miners as well as direct exposure to the underlying metal through trusts. Other ETFs studied here offer pure equity exposure.

The reason I like this blended allocation is that the direct metal helps to mitigate risks such as company-specific risks and may hold value better over the long-run if margins get squeezed for the companies in the future.

Additionally, SLVP tracks more than just silver, as it includes other metals as well, while SLVR may be one of the best 'pure play' ETFs if one has a bullish silver thesis, as it's exclusively tied to the metal.

SLVR's expense ratio is on the higher side relative to the median for most ETFs at 0.65%; however, this is still in line with its peers such as SILJ and SIL, meaning I still believe it is competitively priced when considering its alternatives.

Risks

Although silver prices may have found a stronger price floor due to its structural supply and demand deficits, investors must still be cognizant that the silver market is tiny relative to other global assets or even compared to gold, at one-tenth of its market size. This means that its rallies and sell-offs can come suddenly and be seemingly disconnected from the market fundamentals. A narrow set of traders relative to other industries can change the trajectory of the silver spot price.

Silver has also historically exhibited strong mean-reversion tendencies with sell-offs that flatten its value over the long run to be more in line with its moving average. Therefore, despite the strong rallies and my own optimistic forecasts, I think silver is uniquely risky due to its liquidity pool but not necessarily more so than traditional growth assets. I would not be comfortable having a large allocation in silver at this stage.

Conclusion

Overall, despite the risks, I rate SLVR a buy. The continued supply constraints as well as demand tailwinds are attractive, and with appropriate position sizing, investors can reach an attractive risk-return trade-off. I then reiterate my rating for the ETF and expect good things to come for investors who hold it in the future.

data.title: T. Rowe Price: Structural Issues Aren't Easy To Fix
data.description:
As I covered about two years ago, T. Rowe Price Group (TROW) was suffering from relatively weak investment performance that resulted in net outflows, a trend that was not easy to reverse as the company is largely exposed to active funds, which face structural headwinds as investors are increasingly moving money to passive funds. Despite that, its shares were trading at a premium to peers at the time, which didn’t make much sense in my opinion. Not surprisingly, the performance of its shares has been quite weak over the past couple of years, underperforming the overall stock market by a wide margin.

This weak performance is largely justified by the same trend that TROW was experiencing a couple of years ago, as most issues were structural rather than cyclical. Indeed, as can be seen in the next graph, most of its funds are not outperforming passive alternatives and benchmarks for several historical time periods, with performance being especially negative when considering the 1-year and 3-year periods.

As an active investment management company, the most important distinctive factor of TROW to its competitors is investment performance, as usually investors look for Funds that have historically beat their competitors and respective benchmarks. Given that TROW has a poor track record in recent years across Equities, Fixed Income, and Multi-Asset, it’s not surprising that it has reported net outflows over the past few years.

Indeed, while 2022 was a negative year for capital markets in general, and that could explain net outflows in that particular year, TROW has struggled to be competitive in the asset management industry and continued to report net outflows over the past three years, during a positive period for capital markets.

On the other hand, despite net outflows since 2021 TROW’s assets under management (AuM) were boosted by positive market changes in recent years, reaching a new record high close to $1.8 trillion at the end of last September.

However, despite record high AuMs, the trend of persisting net outflows over the past five years is quite concerning for business growth and raises questions about TROW’s competitive position in the industry. Moreover, considering its weak investment performance in recent years, this is a trend that is not expected to reverse soon. For instance, only 27% of Equity Funds are beating passive alternatives over the last year, and only 19% are above benchmarks, showing a very poor performance that is clearly an important headwind for flows ahead.

Given this backdrop, it’s not surprising to see that TROW had about $8 billion in net outflows during the last quarter (Q3 2025), which was nonetheless an improvement compared to net outflows of nearly $15 billion in the previous quarter. During the first nine months of 2025, its net outflows amounted to more than $31 billion, making it quite likely that net outflows for the full year will be similar to the levels reported last year (net outflows of $43 billion in 2024).

Investors should note that net outflows/inflows are critical in asset management because they represent ‘organic’ growth, or lack of it, while capital market changes are the same for all companies in the industry. Flows are mostly a good indicator to know the level of investor confidence in the company’s product offering, its competitive strength in the industry, and future growth potential. Moreover, asset managers earn revenue based on AuMs and net inflows are an important driver of increasing fee income, while on the other hand, sustained net outflows shrink revenues and earnings over the long term.

Considering that TROW has been reporting net outflows for five consecutive years, during periods of positive and negative returns in the capital markets, this is an important headwind for business growth over the next few years. Given that investment performance has not improved lately, it’s quite likely that TROW will continue to experience net outflows across its three most important asset classes, but more specifically in U.S. large-cap equity Funds that have shown poor performance in the recent past, putting into question its ability to reach its 1-3% organic-growth target in the medium term.

The street also seems to be quite negative about the company’s ability to reverse net outflows over the next two years, given that current consensus is for TROW to have net outflows of about $37 billion in 2026 and around $22 billion in 2027. While there is a high degree of uncertainty about net inflows/outflows ahead, I also think it’s more likely for net outflows to continue as investment performance remains weak and an inflection doesn’t seem likely in the short term.

This expectation is also supported by the company’s AuM mix, which is highly exposed to the domestic market, plus Equities is the largest asset class with close to $900 billion of AuM at the end of last September. Given that TROW is showing poor investment returns in Equities and the U.S. market is the one most affected by the gradually move of investing money away from active to passive funds, this does not bode well for net flows ahead.

From a business strategy perspective, TROW’s profile has not changed much over the past few years, and the company has not made significant efforts, in my opinion, to further diversify its business, even though it has pushed for growth in multi-asset and alternative funds in recent years. However, while competitors like BlackRock (BLK) or Invesco (IVZ) moved their business toward a mix of active and passive funds, TROW maintained its strategy of differentiation through active management, seeking to deliver alpha through internal capabilities of sound portfolio management and in-house research. However, like many of its competitors in the active asset management industry, its track record has not been great in recent years, putting into question the sustainability of its business model over the long term.

Moreover, on top of net outflows that are a structural headwind for its business, the company has also been facing fee compression as competition in the industry is fierce and investors increasingly demand lower fees from active funds, as the differentiation from passive funds has not been great in the recent past. Not surprisingly, TROW’s effective fee rate (EFR) has been declining like it has happened across the industry, from an average level of about 90 basis points (bps) in 2000 (for the U.S. mutual fund industry) to only about 35 bps in 2025. TROW’s effective fee rate was 39 bps in Q3 2025 (without performance fees), which is above average, but declined by about 180 bps during the last year.

This fee compression is clearly negative for the company’s top-line and earnings growth and has a greater impact on its financial performance than net outflows, as rising capital markets were more than enough to boost management fees in recent years.

Given this background, TROW’s revenues amounted to nearly $1.9 billion in Q3 2025, representing annual growth of about 6%. This is justified by an increase of 8.2% on average AuMs in Q3 2025 compared to the same quarter of last year, but its revenue growth rate was lower than AuM growth, showing that TROW’s fee compression is a significant headwind for revenue growth.

Another issue regarding the company’s operating leverage is rising expenses, as the inflationary environment has put pressure on wages and overall administrative expenses. In Q3 2025, TROW’s operating expenses amounted to $1.25 billion, up by 6.7% YoY. Its cost-to-income ratio was 66% in the quarter, which is not fantastic in the asset management industry, and showed a deterioration of more than 30 bps compared to the same quarter of last year.

Its operating income was $643 million in the quarter (+4.9% YoY) and its operating margin declined by 40 bps to 34%, still being a good level of profitability in the industry considering that the average margin is between 30% and 32%.

Going forward, it’s quite likely that TROW will report net outflows in Q4 given that the company reported total AuMs of $1.79 trillion in October (slightly up compared to September) and net outflows of $5.9 billion in the month. The company has recently announced a strategic partnership with Goldman Sachs (GS) aiming to deliver public and private market solutions to customers, with some product launches expected during 2026. This may be an important contribution to an inflection on flows over the coming quarters, but so far it is quite uncertain what the impact of this new partnership will be on the company’s net flows during the next year, and this partnership doesn’t seem to be enough to change its flow trend over the next few quarters.

Regarding its balance sheet, TROW maintains a very strong position with a net cash position of about $4.3 billion, allowing it to return significant capital to shareholders. During the last quarter, TROW returned $442 million through dividends and share buybacks, representing about 70% of its operating income in the period. As the company’s financial position is sound and TROW does not need to retain many earnings ahead, I think its capital return strategy is not likely to change much in the near future, being a combination of a growing dividend and share buybacks.

Its current quarterly dividend is $1.27 per share, which at its current share price leads to a dividend yield of about 4.75%, but looking at current dividend estimates, the street is expecting small dividend growth ahead. While this looks conservative, as TROW’s earnings are increasing at mid-single digits and the company has a sound financial position, I think TROW is likely to raise its dividend like it did last year, probably increasing its quarterly dividend by 2-3% to a level around $1.30 per share when it announces its 2025 earnings at the beginning of next year (probably in February).

Regarding its valuation, TROW is currently trading at around 11x earnings, a much lower valuation compared to when I last analyzed it (around 16x earnings at the time). While TROW was trading at a premium valuation to peers a couple of years ago, the market has de-rated its shares as it became aware that its issues were structural rather than cyclical and that TROW was not likely to reverse its poor investment performance soon, justifying why it’s now trading at a discount to peers (that trade on average at more than 12x earnings). I see this discount as warranted considering that TROW continues to face many issues that aren’t easy to fix, and I’m not expecting an improved operating performance over the next couple of years.

Conclusion

T. Rowe Price doesn’t seem to hold any competitive advantage in the asset management industry, as the company’s strategy to focus on active funds has clearly backfired in recent years, as investment performance is not great across its Funds. This has resulted in net outflows during the past five years, which, together with margin compression and higher expenses, have led to muted earnings growth that was only offset by rising capital markets. Given this backdrop, its shares have de-rated to a lower earnings multiple than in the past and offer a relatively high dividend yield, being the main reasons why I maintain my ‘hold’ rating despite a relatively muted business outlook.

data.title: The Dividend Growth Trifecta: Hunting For Quality, Yield, And Growth
data.description:
Think of a Venn diagram with three circles overlapping in the middle.

One circle is Quality. Another is Yield. The last is Growth (specifically dividend growth). The overlap in the middle is what I like to call the "Dividend Growth Trifecta" — public companies that offer the optimal blend of these three characteristics.

As the S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPY) hovers near its all-time high and sports a forward P/E ratio around 22.5x, it has gotten harder to identify Dividend Compounders within this Venn diagram overlap. But there are still buy-the-dip opportunities.

In my weekly article a few weeks ago, I highlighted two alternative asset managers, Ares Management (ARES) and Blackstone (BX), that struck me as great buying opportunities. Both yielded over 3% and have been growing like weeds. Since then, the two have rallied quite nicely — significantly better than the SPY.

Tactically, when it comes to Dividend Compounders, I've learned that it is better to buy the dip in high-quality, high-growth stalwarts like these two biggies than to bottom-fish in deep value territory for middling-quality or low-growth names.

I think it is best to own the industry leaders, not the 2nd or 3rd or 4th best name in a given industry. Often, the best of the best are too pricey and low-yielding for me. That's okay. I'd rather be patient and wait for the industry leader to go on discount than endure the stress, second-guessing, and sub-par performance of owning a second- or third-tier player.

Here's the agenda for this week:

  • A discussion of the Dividend Growth Trifecta and why I am increasingly focused on owning the core of the Venn diagram.
  • Asking if there might be hope for a near-future rebound in the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF™ (SCHD), which has historically been one of the best "Dividend Growth Trifecta" ETFs on the market.
  • Some charts highlighting the incredible resilience and growth of top-tier stocks this year... despite some erosion in economic fundamentals.
  • Why quality may soon enjoy a comeback, and where I'm hunting for the Trifecta.
  • Some thoughts on my current Dividend Compounder buy list.

Onward!

The Trifecta

Plenty of companies are high-quality but offer pitifully low yields that vastly diminish their appeal to investors who largely measure their investment success in terms of passive income.

Think, for example, of the WisdomTree US Quality Dividend Growth ETF (DGRW), which does a fantastic job of capturing high-quality stocks that paya dividend. But top holdings include several Big Tech stocks yielding well under 1%, which overwhelmingly gears the ETF toward price appreciation rather than income.

On the other hand, a great many stocks offer attractive yields but little to no growth and questionable quality. For example, the SPDR S&P 500 High Dividend ETF (SPYD) holds a basket of 80 dividend payers averaging about a 4.5% yield, but this fund has produced next to zero dividend growth over the last decade and holds plenty of stocks that are (in my view) of dubious quality.

Finally, there are a small group of stocks offering attractive yields and impressive growth but poor quality, especially in terms of balance sheet strength. I have admittedly owned my fair share of these types of companies and been burned by too many of them.

For example, XLPR Infrastructure LP (XIFR), the former NextEra Energy Partners, was a favorite of mine several years ago. In my last (buy-rated) article on it in July 2022, I interpreted a cash preservation move by management as a bullish signal but ignored the tsunami of interest expense increases headed its way — penny-wise but pound-foolish.

The debt-heavy, junk-rated subsidiary of NextEra Energy Inc. (NEE) got absolutely crushed by rising interest rates:

Notice that XIFR only got crushed after years of very solid performance—performance that, in retrospect, was heavily dependent on low interest rates and cheap capital.

I would have been far better off simply owning NEE, the lower-yielding parent company. While XIFR completely suspended its dividend, NEE has continued to grow it at a double-digit pace.

Such is the danger of compromising on quality for the sake of a nice yield+growth profile.

On the surface, Clearway Energy Inc. (CWEN, CWEN.A) looks similar to XIFR in a lot of ways. It yields well over 5% and has been growing its dividend at a ~7% annual pace but carries a large debt load and sports a BB credit rating. The critical difference between CWEN and XIFR, I think, is in how the balance sheet is structured. XIFR made ample use of both floating rate debt and convertible equity capital, whereas CWEN's management has hedged all floating rate debt, spaced out its corporate bonds, and avoided convertible equity altogether.

The bottom line here is that the trifecta of quality, yield, and growth is the sweet spot for dividend growth investors, and that is where I want to focus my investment capital.

Hope For SCHD?

Up until a few years ago, one could very easily and successfully invest in the Divided Growth Trifecta with one click simply by buying SCHD.

I've discussed what went wrong with SCHD, especially at its most recent reconstitution in March, in past articles and won't rehash that here.

Suffice it to say that after SCHD's late-March 2025 reconstitution, its two largest sectoral holdings became upstream energy (XOP) and consumer staples (XLP), both of which have significantly underperformed this year.

For a while, staples traded sideways while upstream energy languished, but recently the latter has made something of a comeback, which has given SCHD a small boost. That's a bit baffling to me because the price of crude oil has remained around $60 per barrel. Controlling costs, rising natural gas prices, and stock buybacks have helped producers.

While I continue to hold a large position in SCHD, the ETF remains off my buy list for now, because I just don't see a path for it to return to its double-digit dividend growth algorithm previously achieved from about 2012-2022.

There's no shortage of oil and gas, and although the producers in SCHD's portfolio have decent yields and quality metrics, their growth prospects are dim.

The Incredible Resilience of Top-Tier US Stocks

The best performing U.S. stocks this year have been characterized by fantastic growth but varying quality and ultra-low or zero yields.

And while I am still concerned about a valuation overshoot in the AI-tech ecosystem, I must admit that the high stock price appreciation in this space has been largely driven by earnings growth.

To be clear, I think this AI-tech earnings growth largely derives from three places:

  1. Efficiency-related cost reductions.
  2. Non-AI-related growth in Big Tech such as cloud storage and advertising.
  3. A circular transfer of cash from the hyperscalers to chipmakers to software developers and back into hyperscalers.

As such, I still worry that the market is being duped into thinking that SPY returns this year are driven by strong returns on AI investments when they're really not. I've seen no evidence (and I've looked!) that AI software ROI is improving to the point where it will become materially profitable in the foreseeable future.

Yes, AI revenues are growing, but so is AI capex.

These investments could turn out to be profitable for the AI software developers. I'm just saying the evidence to support this idea looks thin to me.

I don't think investors are very good at holding space in their minds for two very different possibilities—(1) that the AI programs themselves aren't going to be profitable enough to justify massive investment anytime soon and (2) that AI programs will be increasingly profitable in the coming years, more or less justifying current investments. Everyone convinces themselves of one or the other, typically based on what they're already invested in.

In any case, it appears as though earnings growth is going to continue in the coming years, driven by the three points listed above, whether AI software turns out to be profitable or not.

And as I've mentioned in the past, it makes sense to me that the SPY would trade at a higher-than-average valuation multiple because of its higher-than-average profit margin of almost 14%.

Think about that. For every dollar of revenue that comes into S&P 500 companies, 14 cents drops down to the bottom line. That compares to about 10 cents in 2005-2006 and 12 cents right before the beginning of the pandemic.

As Josh Brown of CNBC and Ritholz Wealth Management likes to say, "Compares have just become better at being companies over time" (not an exact quote).

Surely that is good news, even if the level of earnings growth is being inflated right now by the circular flow of spending within the AI ecosystem.

But here's more bad news: There are still plenty of signs that the non-AI economy is floundering. You can see that in the frozen housing market, weak wage growth, and sliding private sector job growth.

According to ADP, small businesses have been shedding jobs all year. Mid-sized businesses were meagerly hiring early in the year but have since turned to a mildly net negative change in payrolls. Only big businesses are still hiring.

In a normal economy, this would be unequivocally bad news.

But in an incredibly top-heavy and AI-driven market, the only two questions investors seemingly need to ask are:

  1. Are affluent Americans continuing to spend?
  2. Are tech companies continuing to build data centers and buy chips?

If the answer to those two questions continues to be "Yes," then it seems that the bull market will continue, regardless what happens elsewhere in the economy.

Hunting For Quality, Yield, and Growth in Today's Market

My quest is always the same. It is to find and invest in the trifecta described above.

Now, to be clear, my sphere of proficiency is in commercial real estate, which is why I focus most of my individual stock-picking in this sector. But even when investing in ETFs, the target I aim for is the Dividend Growth Trifecta.

On that note, there is more (potential) good news.

Let's isolate the quality factor. In the long run, quality stocks tend to outperform low-quality stocks. But during certain risk-on periods (such as 2021), quality stocks can significantly underperform.

Notice in the chart below how quality stocks hugely underperformed at the height of the dot com bubble in the late-1990s:

But notice also that when reality didn't meet inflated expectations and caused the bubble to burst, quality stocks massively outperformed low-quality stocks in the early 2000s.

While it's always hard to predict that history is going to repeat or "rhyme," I'd be willing to bet my own money (literally) that quality stocks are going to begin outperforming again soon.

But where in particular am I looking for high-quality stocks today?

Aside from my area of specialty in commercial real estate and REITs, I'm particularly interested in two sectors: Utilities and consumer staples.

I like utilities because for the first time in almost two decades, electricity demand is projected to enjoy robust and sustained growth.

This provides ample investment opportunities for electric utilities to grow their rate bases and bump up their earnings growth rates into the high-single-digits.

Combine that with dividend yields of around 3.0% to 3.5% and you've got a pretty compelling dividend growth opportunity.

The other sector I like is consumer staples. While I think low population growth is a distinct headwind, two other factors increase my interest in staples. First, staples are highly defensive, and the higher quality ones should perform well if AI reality doesn't meet AI expectations. Second, right now insiders are demonstrating more bullishness via open-market share purchases in the staples space than at any time going back to the Great Financial Crisis.

While I'm not wild about consumer staples as a sector, this level of insider confidence is eyebrow-raising.

My Dividend Growth Buy List Today

Turning now to my buy list, I'll note that I've got two REITs, a small water utility, and a dividend ETF on my radar.

Dividend Yield Projected Dividend Growth Rate (Guesstimate)
American Homes 4 Rent (AMH) 3.8% Mid-Single-Digit
American Tower (AMT) 3.8% Mid- to High-Single-Digit
Artesian Resources (ARTNA) 4.0% Low- to Mid-Single-Digit
VictoryShares US Lg Cap High Dividend Vol-Weighted ETF (CDL) 3.3% Mid-Single-Digit

It's just happenstance that all three of the individual stocks start with the letter "A."

American Homes 4 Rent

I believe AMH is being dragged down by poor labor market data. Many markets across the country are absorbing a 50-year high in apartment completions right now, and low job growth is not helping landlords like AMH. Many Millennials would like to move into a single-family home, even if as a renter, but usually getting a new job is the catalyst for such an upgrade.

But the good news for AMH is that the affordability gap between buying and renting remains massive and isn't expected to close anytime soon. One recent analysis found that mortgage rates would need to drop to 3.7% to create cost parity between buying and renting, but forecasts expect mortgage rates to decline only slightly going forward.

On average, renting is over $500 per month cheaper than buying a single-family home today. Despite the obvious pent-up demand to buy homes, the math is the math. Would-be homebuyers will likely to continue renting and eventually opt to upgrade into an SFR in lieu of a narrowing of the affordability gap.

American Tower

AMT, along with its tower REIT peers, has been languishing amid headwinds from the contract dispute with EchoStar.

A recent analyst note from Barclays suggests that the tower REITs legally have the upper hand but may still have to settle for less than the contractual revenue they are owed. While an unfortunate setback, I view this as small potatoes in the grand scheme, in which mobile data usage and telecommunications infrastructure investment will continue to grow meaningfully in the years to come.

I'm also impressed by AMT's cost of debt, as exemplified by its recent issuance of $850 million in 7-year bonds at an interest rate of 4.7%. AMT's strong balance sheet is a major element of its quality.

Artesian Resources

I had built a small to mid-sized position in ARTNA earlier this year, reached a nice round number, and wanted to stop buying. But recently, ARTNA has dipped further to the point where it now yields 4%, and I said to myself, "Ah, what the heck, I'll buy some more."

The small water utility's Q3 results were nothing out of the ordinary. Revenue grew 4.6% YoY while EPS rose 3.0%. Year to date, revenue increased 4.7%, and EPS increased 12.4%, rebounding off of an earnings dip last year.

Debt is modest, the payout ratio is around 60%, returns on equity consistently track around 9-10%, the dividend has grown (slowly) for 32 consecutive years, and the founding Taylor family continues to manage the company conservatively.

ARTNA's biggest sin is being boring at a time when growthy tech stocks are all the rage.

CDL ETF

Remember the two sectors I highlighted as being particularly interesting for dividend growth investors looking to invest in the trifecta today? CDL's two largest sectors of utilities and staples provide ample exposure to the trifecta right now, in my view.

46% of CDL's portfolio is in these two sectors, which together represent a good blend of quality, yield, and growth.

I'm guesstimating dividend growth in the mid-single-digits per year going forward, which combines nicely with the ETF's 3.3% yield.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: JEPQ: Fund Inflow Slows And Alpha Potential Weakens (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
JEPQ ETF: Previous Thesis And New Catalysts

I last covered the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) on 10.9 in an article titled “JEPQ Vs. QQQ: The Case For Covered-Call Funds In 2 Charts”. The article explained why, at that time, the option strategy of JEPQ offered a more attractive return/risk profile than its underlying index approximated by the Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ). As such, I gave JEPQ a buy rating. Since then, there have been a few new catalysts evolving around the ETF, both in terms of fund specifics and also macroeconomic conditions. In the remainder of this article, I will focus on the top catalyst in each category: JEPQ’s latest fund flow data and the implied volatility of its underlying index. These developments have led me to see weakened alpha potential for JEPQ and to downgrade my rating to hold.

Let me start with fund flow data. The chart below displays the JEPQ net fund flows over the last three months. Note that the net flows are measured in millions of dollars ($M). Flows have fluctuated significantly with alternating inflow and outflow patterns. The largest single-day outflow approached almost $200M. Overall, the fund has experienced a positive net inflow of $635 M. Compared to the fund’s AUM of $32 billion, the overall inflow is quite small (less than 2% of the total AUM) and severely slowed compared to the inflow in earlier periods. . As detailed next, I believe such a slowed inflow foretells weakened alpha potential ahead.

JEPQ ETF: Basic fund facts

Before going any further, let’s take a quick detour to cover some basic fund facts for investors unfamiliar with JEPQ yet. The JEPQ ETF is an actively managed fund based on the Nasdaq 100 index and is thus categorized as a nontraditional equity fund as seen in the chart below. Its active strategy is largely reflected in two ways: A) it uses a proprietary algorithm to pick its holdings from the Nasdaq 100 universe, and B) it also uses an option overlay to generate monthly income. More details of its strategy are quoted from its fund fact sheet below.

JEPQ Fund description: JEPQ actively invests in US large-cap stocks comprising its benchmark, the Nasdaq-100 Index, while pursuing lower volatility. The fund may also invest up to 20% of the portfolio in equity-linked notes (ELNs) issued by counterparties, including banks, broker-dealers or their affiliates, to provide additional income.

As detailed in my earlier articles, I believe the fund’s strategy is fundamentally sound. Also, a 0.35% expense ratio is quite reasonable for an actively managed fund. As a reflection of these strengths, the fund has delivered satisfactory results since its inception in 2022 and has accumulated a sizable AUM of more than $32 billion in a bit more than ~2 years since then. In contrast, its sister fund (JEPI) took more than 5 years to attract about $41 billion AUM.

JEPQ Fund Flow In Focus

However, the fund’s momentum has slowed considerably in the recent few months, judging by its fund flow data. As aforementioned in the first section, in the most recent three months (roughly between Sept and Nov), the fund has experienced a small positive net inflow of $635 M, representing less than 2% of its current AUM. In contrast, in the past 6 months, the fund has attracted a far larger positive net inflow of $2.4 billion, as shown in the next chart. These figures imply that the fund attracted about $1.8 billion in the 3 months between June and August, roughly tripling its inflow in the last 3 months.

Looking ahead, I see such slowed inflow continuing, and consequently the fund’s alpha potential weakening for several reasons. To start, the valuation for its underlying universe (the NASDAQ 100 index) has become considerably more expensive lately. As an example, the next chart below shows the P/E and CAPE ratios for the index per data from Siblis Research. Based on Siblis’ most recent updates, the CAPE ratio of NASDAQ 100 was 52.56x as of June 30.

Compared to the reading of 34.20x about ~3 years ago, this represents a large expansion of 53.5%. Since June, the Nasdaq 100 index has further gained about 13% as of this writing. Thus, as a crude estimate, the index’s current CAPE ratio is more than 60% above its level ~3 years ago. Even compared to the reading in Q1 of 2025 (44.93x as seen), the CAPE ratio has expanded by almost 30% in my estimate. Such a sizable—and rapid—valuation expansion has materially diminished JEPQ’s ability to pick its holdings with meaningful alpha in my view.

Other Risks And Final Thoughts

Besides the elevated valuation, I also expect the income generation potential of JEPQ to be weakened in the near future, judging by the implied volatility (IV) of the QQQ ETF in the option market. As an example, the following statistics were taken from MarketChameleon. As seen, they reveal that the current IV of QQQ is only 18.1, lower than its 52-week average of 20.0 by about 10%. The current IV of 18.1 is also -18.2% below its 20-day moving average of 22.2, indicating a downward trend. Finally, compared to QQQ HV (historical or realized volatility), the current IV is about 19% below the 20-day HV (22.4) and about 14% below the 252-day HV of 21.2.

In the past year, QQQ has experienced extreme volatilities (as high as 47.5) largely due to the tariff disputes the US started with its trading partners. Such extreme volatilities have provided a favorable pricing environment for JEPQ’s option strategy to support a lucrative dividend. At this point, I believe the tariff uncertainties have mostly dissipated and expect relatively muted IV in the near future, which will translate into an unfavorable pricing environment for JEPQ’s option strategy.

To close, this JEPQ revisit is largely triggered by JEPQ’s latest fund flow data and the implied volatility of its underlying index. After examining these developments, my conclusion is that JEPQ’s positives and negatives are closely balanced under the current valuation and volatility parameters. These considerations have led me to see a neutral reward/risk ratio and adjust my rating to hold.

data.title: 5 Relatively Secure And Cheap Dividend Stocks, Yields Up To 8% (December 2025)
data.description:
************

Author's Note: This is our monthly series on Dividend Stocks, usually published in the first week of every month. We scan the universe of roughly 7,500 stocks listed and traded on U.S. exchanges and use our proprietary filtering criteria to select five relatively safe stocks that may be trading cheaper compared to their historical valuations. Some of the sections in the article, like "Selection Process/Methodology," are repeated each month with few changes and are presented in "italics." This is intentional as well as unavoidable, as this is necessary for the new readers to be able to conceptualize the process. Regular readers of this series could skip such sections to avoid repetitiveness and jump to the section titled "Selection of the Top 50." Readers can also read a more detailed explanation of the goals and the selection process in our blog post here.

************

Introduction and Market Summary:

After an extended rally and making fresh all-time highs for several months, the market has stalled somewhat. In fact, it has been trading in a tight range between S&P 500 (SPX) levels 6600 and 6900. It is not far from the psychologically important level of SPX 7000. There is a fierce debate and tug of war going on between the bulls and some prominent bears right now. Also, the earnings season is already over, and there is no new catalyst to push the stocks to the next level.

However, as we enter the month of December, generally highly positive for stocks, all eyes are now on the next FOMC (Fed's Open Market Committee) meeting scheduled for the 10th of December. Whether the Fed delivers the widely expected next rate cut or not is yet to be seen. In fact, the Fed itself is highly divided on the direction of the rates. In fact, the Fed has acknowledged its strongly differing views across the rate-setting committee members. If we do get the next rate cut on Dec. 10th, it is not difficult to see the markets cross SPX 7000 by the time the year is out, despite some concerns of an AI-led bubble.

You can see the current odds of the Fed's next rate cut here on the CME FedWatchTool website.

It is always good to have some idea of where the markets and the broader economy may be headed. Even so, we believe markets are always unpredictable, and it is not possible to catch the exact bottom (or the peak). So, it is best to invest regularly and consistently in good, solid dividend-paying stocks when their valuations are reasonable or attractive.

This month, we highlight three groups with five stocks each that have an average dividend yield (as a group) of 3.6%, 6.2%, and 8.6%, respectively. The first list is for conservative and risk-averse investors, while the second is for investors seeking higher yields but still wanting relatively safe dividends. The third group is for yield-hungry investors but comes with an elevated risk, and we urge investors to exercise caution.

The Selection Process:

Goals and Objectives:

The main goal of this series of articles is to shortlist and highlight companies that have a solid history of paying and raising dividends. In addition, we demand that these companies support strong fundamentals, carry low debt, and are offered at a relatively cheaper valuation. These DGI stocks are not going to make anyone rich overnight, but if your goal is to attain financial freedom by owning stocks that would grow dividends over time, meaningfully and sustainably, then you are at the right place. These lists are not necessarily recommendations to buy, but a shortlist of probable candidates for further research. Besides, we think each month, if nothing else, this analysis is able to highlight some nuggets that would have otherwise skipped our attention.

Every month, we start with roughly 7,500 stocks that are listed and traded on U.S. exchanges, including over-the-counter (OTC) networks. Using our filtering criteria, the initial list is quickly narrowed to roughly 700 stocks, mostly dividend-paying and dividend-growing. From thereon, by using various data elements, including dividend history, payout ratios, revenue growth, debt ratios, EPS growth, etc., we calculate a "Dividend Quality Score" for each stock that measures the relative safety and sustainability of the dividend. In addition to dividend safety, we also seek cheaper valuations. We also demand that the selected companies have an established business model, solid dividend history, manageable debt, and investment-grade credit rating.

Notes: 1) Please note that when we use the term "safe" in relation to stocks and investments, it should be interpreted as "relatively safe" because nothing is absolutely safe in investing. Even though we present only 5 to 10 stocks in our final list, one should have 15-20 stocks at a minimum in a well-diversified portfolio.

2) All tables in this article are created by the author unless explicitly specified. The stock data have been sourced from various sources such as Seeking Alpha, Yahoo Finance, GuruFocus, Morningstar, and CCC-List (Dividend Radar).

Criteria to Shortlist:

  • Market cap > $7 billion (some exceptions can be made)
  • Dividend yield > 1.0% (some exceptions are made to include high-quality but lower-yielding companies)
  • Daily average volume > 100,000
  • Dividend growth past five years >= 0.

By applying the above criteria, we got around 600 companies.

Narrowing Down the List

As a first step, we would like to eliminate stocks that have less than five years of dividend history. Also, since we want a lot of flexibility and wider choice at this initial stage, we include some companies that pay dividends lower than the S&P500 but otherwise have a stellar dividend record and growing dividends at a fast pace.

However, so far in this list, we have demanded five or more years of consistent dividend growth. But what if a company had a very stable record of dividend payments but did not increase the dividends from one year to another? At times, some of these companies are foreign-based companies, and due to currency fluctuations, their dividends may appear to have been cut in U.S. dollars, but in reality, that may not be true at all when looked at in the actual currency of reporting. At times, we may provide some exceptions when a company may have cut the dividend in the past, but otherwise, it looks compelling.

We now import the various data elements from many sources and assign weights based on different criteria as listed below:

  • Current yield: Indicates the yield based on the current price.
  • Dividend growth history (number of years of dividend growth): This provides information on how many years a company has paid and increased dividends on a consistent basis. For stocks under the category 'B' (defined above), we consider the total number of consecutive years of dividends paid rather than the number of years of dividend growth.
  • Payout ratio: This indicates how comfortably the company can pay the dividend from its earnings. We prefer this ratio to be as low as possible, which would indicate the company's ability to grow the dividend in the future. This ratio is calculated by dividing the dividend amount per share by the EPS (earnings per share). The cash-flow payout ratio is calculated by dividing the dividend amount paid per share by the cash flow generated per share.
  • Past three-year and 5-year dividend growth: Even though it's the dividend growth rate from the past, this does indicate how fast the company has been able to grow its earnings and dividends in the recent past. The recent past is the best indicator that we have to know what to expect in the next few years.
  • EPS growth (average of previous five years of growth and expected next five years' growth): As the earnings of a company grow, more than likely, dividends will grow accordingly. We will take into account the actual EPS growth of the previous five years and the estimated EPS growth for the next five years. We will add the two numbers and assign weights.
  • Chowder number: So, what's the Chowder number? This number has been named after the well-known SA author Chowder, who first coined and popularized this factor. This number is derived by adding the current yield and the past five years' dividend growth rate. A Chowder number of "12" or more ("8" for utilities) is considered good.
  • Debt/equity ratio: This ratio will tell us about the company's debt load in relation to its equity. We all know that too much debt can lead to major problems, even for well-known companies. The lower this ratio, the better it is. Sometimes, we find this ratio to be negative or unavailable, even for well-known companies. This can happen for a myriad of reasons and is not always a reason for concern. This is why we use this ratio in combination with the debt/asset ratio (covered next).
  • Debt/asset ratio: This ratio will tell us about the debt load in relation to the company's total assets. In almost all cases, this ratio would be lower than the debt/equity ratio. Also, this ratio is important because, for some companies, the debt/equity ratio is not a reliable indicator.
  • S&P's credit rating: This is the credit rating assigned by the rating agency S&P Global and is indicative of the company's ability to service its debt. This rating can be obtained from the S&P website.
  • PEG ratio: This is also called the price/earnings-to-growth ratio. The PEG ratio is considered to be an indicator if the stock is overvalued, undervalued, or fairly priced. A lower PEG may indicate that a stock is undervalued. However, PEG for a company may differ significantly from one reported source to another, depending on which growth estimate is used in the calculation. Some use past growth, while others may use future expected growth.
  • Distance from 52-week high: We want to select companies that are good, solid companies, but also are trading at cheaper valuations currently. They may be cheaper due to some temporary down cycle, or some combination of bad news, or simply having a bad quarter. This criterion will help bring such companies (with a cheaper valuation) near the top as long as they excel in other criteria as well. This factor is calculated as (current price - 52-week high) / 52-week high.
  • Sales or Revenue growth: This is the average growth rate in annual sales or revenue of the company over the last five years. A company can only grow its earnings power as long as it can grow its revenue. Sure, it can grow the earnings by cutting costs, but that can't go on forever.

Selection Of The Top 50

Downloadable Dataset: Below, we provide a link to the table with relevant data on nearly 500 stocks. Readers can download this table for further analysis. Please note that the table is sorted by "Total Weight" or "Quality Score."

File-for-export_-_5_Best_DGI_-_DEC_2025.xlsx

We will first bring down the list (of 400-plus names) to roughly 50 names by automated criteria, as listed below. In the second step, which is mostly manual and subjective, we will bring the list down to about 15.

  • Step 1: First, take the top 20 names from the above table (based on total weight or quality score). At times, some industry segments tend to get overcrowded at the top, so we take the top two (or three) and ignore the rest.
  • Step 2: As a second step, we will take the top 10 names based on the highest dividend yield. When it comes to dividend yield, some of the industry segments tend to be overcrowded. So, we will take the top two (or a maximum of three) names from any single industry segment.
  • Step 3: Now, we will sort our list based on five-year dividend growth (highest at the top) and select the top 10 names.
  • Step 4: We will now consider more recent dividend growth, as that is more indicative of the company's future growth. We will sort our list based on 3-year dividend growth (highest at the top) and select the top 10 names.
  • Step 5: We also want to give priority to stocks that are rated highest in terms of credit rating. So, we will sort the list based on the numerical weight of the credit rating and select the top 10 stocks with the best credit rating. Again, we are careful not to have too many names from the same sector.
  • Step 6: Lastly, as the name of the series suggests, we want to have some names that may be trading cheaper in comparison to their historical valuation. So, we select the top ten names with the highest discount. However, they could be trading cheap for just the wrong reasons, so we need to be careful that they meet our other quality criteria.

From the above steps, we now have a total of 70 names in our final consideration. However, the following stocks appeared more than once:

Stocks that appeared two times: ARE, AU, CI, COKE, GE, IBKR, LYB, OC, TJX, V, and WIT (11 duplicates).

Stocks that appeared three times: CIB, HWM (4 duplicates).

After removing eight duplicates, we are left with 55 (70-15) names.

Since we still have a very large list, we will remove names that have a low yield and not very great dividend growth. So, if the dividend yield was less than < 2.5% and the dividend growth (average of 5-year and 10-year) was less than 10%, we would rather remove them at this stage.

So, we remove WMT and JPM. After removing them, we are left with 53 names.

Since there are still multiple names in each industry segment, we will keep a maximum of two or three names (from the top) from any one segment.

On this basis, we remove a total of 13 names: WLK, BAH, WIT, MSFT, ACN, MPWR, NKE, COKE, TRGP, CI, NVO, LLY, and NDSN.

After we remove these 15 (2+13) names, we are left with 40 (55-15) names.

The top names from each sector/industry segment are presented:

Financial Services, Banking, and Insurance:

Banks- Regional:

Banks- Major:

Bank (Foreign) – (CIB)

Financial Services - Others: (IBKR), (ARCC).

Security & Exchanges –

Insurance: (AEG), (OTCQX:ZURVY).

(V), (ADP)

(CSL)

(TS), (PAC), (GE)

(OTCPK:AMKBY).

Materials/Chemicals/Mining/Gold:

Chemicals: (LYB)

Materials - others:

Tobacco: (MO).

(CMCSA), (TU).

Healthcare:

Healthcare Services:

Pipelines/ Midstream: (MPLX).

Refining:

(ATO)
